Ayahs

عَمَّ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (١)
What is it that they are questioning each other about?! (1)
عَنِ ٱلنَّبَإِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢)
[Is it] about the Great Tiding, (2)
ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ (٣)
the one about which they differ? (3)
كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
No indeed! Soon they will know! (4)
ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ (٥)
No indeed! Soon they will know for once again! (5)

Content of Surah An-Naba

In principle, the vast majority of the Surahs in the final part (Juz') of the Quran were revealed in "Makkah." More than anything else, they emphasize the issues of the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), as well as glad tidings and warnings, which is the nature of Makkan Surahs. They often possess a striking, soul-shaking, and awakening tone. The verses, except in a few cases, are short and full of allusions. For this reason, they leave a very deep impact on every aware individual, awaken the unaware, breathe life into soulless bodies, and grant a sense of commitment and responsibility to indifferent individuals. This part of the Quran has a world of its own—a world full of clamor, passion, and resonance! Surah An-Naba is no exception to this general principle; it begins with an awakening question and ends with a sentence full of lessons.

The content of this Surah can be summarized into several sections:

  1. The question raised at the beginning of the Surah regarding the great event (the Great News), namely the Day of Resurrection.

  2. It then proceeds to describe examples of the manifestations of Allah's power in the heavens, the earth, the lives of human beings, and their blessings—as evidence for the possibility of the Resurrection and the Rising.

  3. In another section, it describes some of the signs of the beginning of the Resurrection.

  4. In another part, it depicts a glimpse of the painful punishments of the transgressors.

  5. Following that, it describes a portion of the soul-stirring blessings and favors of Paradise.

  6. Finally, the Surah concludes with a severe warning of a near punishment, followed by a mention of the tragic fate of the disbelievers.

Incidentally, the naming of this Surah is due to the expression found in its second verse, and it is sometimes referred to as Surah "Amma" in accordance with its first verse.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah An-Naba

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Amma Yatasa'alun,' Allah will quench his thirst with a cool and pleasant heavenly drink on the Day of Resurrection." [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever continues to recite 'Amma Yatasa'alun' every day, a year will not pass until he visits the Sacred House (the Kaaba)!" [^2] Furthermore, it is narrated in another Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"Whoever recites it and memorizes it, his accounting on the Day of Resurrection will be (so swift that it will be) as long as the duration of a single prayer." [^3]

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 420.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 419.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ (1) عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ (2) الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ (3) كَلاَّ سَيَعْلَمُونَ (4)

ثُمَّ كَلاَّ سَيَعْلَمُونَ (5)

Commentary

The Great News!

In the first verse of the Surah, as an inquiry mixed with wonder, He says:

"About what are they asking one another?" (عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ) [^1]

Then, without waiting for their answer, He proceeds to answer Himself, adding: "They are asking about the great and momentous news" (عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ‌).

"The very news about which they are constantly in disagreement" (الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ‌). Regarding what is meant by this "Great News" (al-Naba' al-Azim), commentators have offered several answers: some consider it a reference to the Day of Resurrection; some to the revelation of the Magnificent Quran; some to all the principles of religion from Monotheism to the Resurrection; and in some narrations, it is interpreted as the issue of Wilayah (Guardianship) and Imamate, which will be mentioned in the upcoming points. A careful study of the entirety of the verses of this Surah—especially the expressions in the subsequent verses and the sentence "Indeed, the Day of Judgment is an appointed time" which comes after mentioning the signs of Allah's power in the earth and sky, and considering the fact that the most intense opposition of the polytheists was regarding the issue of "Resurrection"—collectively supports the first interpretation, namely the Resurrection and the Rising.

"Naba'," according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, refers to news that is "important," possesses "benefit," and regarding which a person attains "knowledge" or "predominant opinion" (Zann-e Ghalib); these three elements are conditions for the meaning of Naba'. [^1] Therefore, the description as "Azim" (Great) conveys even more emphasis. Altogether, it shows that this news, which a group doubted, was a reality that was known, highly significant, and grand. As we said, the most appropriate meaning is the news of the Resurrection.

The phrase "Yatasa'alun" (they ask one another) might refer only to the disbelievers, who were constantly questioning each other about the Resurrection, not for the sake of research or understanding the truth.

There is also the possibility that it refers to them asking the believers, or asking the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself. [^2] A question arises here: if the "Great News" refers to the Resurrection, and this matter was apparently denied by all disbelievers, why does He say: "they are in disagreement about it"?

In response, we say: Firstly, the denial of the Resurrection in an absolute sense was not certain even among the polytheists, as many of them accepted the survival of the soul after the body—or in other words, a spiritual resurrection—in general.

[^1]: "Amma" is a contraction of "An-ma," composed of "An" (about) and the interrogative "Ma" (what).

[^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, under the root "Naba'."

[^2]: It should be noted that the form "Tafa'ul," although usually meaning an action performed reciprocally, in some cases carries the meaning of the simple triliteral verb or other meanings. (Some linguists have mentioned five meanings for Tafa'ul: 1. Participation of two or more people in an action. 2. Compliance, like "taba'ud." 3. Pretending something without reality, like "tamaruḍ" [feigning illness]. 4. Gradual occurrence, like "tawarud." 5. The meaning of the simple triliteral verb, like "ta'ala" which means "ala" [to be high]).

However, regarding the physical resurrection, some expressed doubt and hesitation, which the tone of the Quranic verses reflects (An-Naml: 66), while others were vehemently in denial and even considered the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (God forbid) insane or a fabricator against Allah for claiming physical resurrection (Saba: 7 and 8). Thus, their disagreement regarding the issue of the Resurrection cannot be denied.

Then He adds: "It is not as they say and think about the Resurrection, and soon they will know" (كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ‌). [^1]

"Again, it is not as they imagine, and soon they will become aware" (ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ‌). "On that day, they will become aware when their cries of 'O my grief!' are raised, and they will deeply regret their negligence and shortcomings": "Lest a soul should say, 'O my grief over what I neglected in regard to Allah'" (Az-Zumar: 56).

On that day when the waves of punishment surround them, and they request to return to the world: "Is there any way to return?" (Ash-Shura: 44).

Even at the moment of death, when the veils are removed from before man's eyes,

[^1]: It is well-known among scholars of Arabic literature and commentators that "Kalla" is a particle of deterrence (rad''), and its meaning is negation or prohibition regarding what preceded it. However, some have said it is rarely used in other meanings, naming three: "emphasis," the introductory "Ala," and a "particle of response equivalent to 'Yes'" (Na'am), each of which has been chosen by some (Majma' al-Bahrayn and other books).

and the realities of the other world become manifest before him, and he attains certainty in the Purgatory (Barzakh) and the Resurrection, at that very moment his cry will be raised: "Send me back so that I may do righteous deeds in that which I left behind": "My Lord, send me back, that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind" (Al-Mu'minun: 99-100).

The use of "Sa-ya'lamun" (with the prefix "Sa" which usually denotes the near future) indicates that the Resurrection is a near event, and the entire life of this world is no more than an hour in comparison to it! Regarding whether the two above verses, which appear as a repetition, are for the purpose of emphasizing one reality (their awareness in the near future of the Resurrection) or to state two separate matters (the first referring to seeing the punishment of this world in the near future, and the second referring to seeing the punishment of the Hereafter after that), commentators have offered two possibilities, but the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

The possibility has also been raised that it means that with the progress of human science and knowledge, the evidence and proofs for the existence of the Resurrection will become so abundant that even the deniers will find no choice but to confess to it.

However, the flaw in this interpretation is that such awareness would be for future generations of mankind, not for the group that lived during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and disagreed about the Resurrection, while the verse is speaking about them.

Key Points

1. The Issue of "Wilayah" and the "Great News":

As we mentioned, "al-Naba' al-Azim" has been interpreted in several ways: the Resurrection, the Quran, or all the principles of religious belief including the Origin and the Resurrection. However, the existing clues in the collection of verses of this Surah show that its interpretation as "Resurrection" is superior to all.

Nevertheless, in many narrations transmitted through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام and some Sunni channels, "al-Naba' al-Azim" (the Great News) has been interpreted as the issue of the Wilayah and Imamate of the Commander of the Faithful, "Ali" عليه السلام, which was a subject of disagreement and discussion by a group, or as the issue of "Wilayah in general."

These narrations are sometimes quoted from Ali عليه السلام himself, and sometimes from other Imams عليه السلام. As an example, we bring three narrations here:

  1. A narration cited by Hafiz Muhammad ibn Mu'min al-Shirazi, who is a Sunni scholar, that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said in the interpretation of "About what are they asking one another? About the Great News": "It refers to the Wilayah of Ali, about which they will be questioned in the grave. No one in the East or West of the world, on land or sea, passes away except that the angels ask him about the Wilayah of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام after death, saying to him: 'What is your religion? Who is your Prophet? And who is your Imam?'" [^1]

  2. In another Hadith, it is mentioned that on the day of the Battle of Siffin, a man from the Syrian army, wearing armor and carrying a Quran, entered the battlefield reciting the Surah "About what are they asking one another? About the Great News." Ali عليه السلام personally went out to him and said: "Do you know what the Great News is, about which they disagree?" The man replied: "No, I do not know!" The Imam said:

"By Allah, I am the Great News about which you disagree! And regarding whose Wilayah you have contended, and from whose Wilayah you turned back after you had accepted it... and on the Day of Resurrection, you will know what you [already] knew!" [^1]

  1. In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that he said:

"The Great News is the Wilayah." [^2]

Reconciling the content of these narrations with what we mentioned regarding the interpretation of the verse as the Resurrection is possible in two ways: First, "al-Naba' al-Azim" has a broad and expansive concept that includes all of these, even though at the time of the revelation of these verses, the Quran's emphasis was primarily on the Resurrection. However, this does not prevent the verse from having other instances (misdaq). Second, as we know and have stated many times, the Quran has various layers (butun); that is, a verse may have multiple meanings, among which one is the apparent (zahir) meaning, and others are the inner (batn) meanings of the Quran, which are derived with the help of various clues, or in other words, a type of "implied indication" (dalalat iltizami) that is not clear to everyone except the elite.

It is not only this verse that has an apparent and an inner meaning; we have many other verses in the Holy Quran for which various interpretations have come in Islamic narrations, some of which harmonize with the apparent meaning, while others express the inner meaning.

However, we emphatically remind that understanding the inner meaning of the Quran is not permissible without the existence of clear clues or interpretations that have reached us from the Prophet himself and the Infallible Imams عليه السلام. The existence of inner layers for the Quran must not become a pretext for the self-indulgent and the deviant to interpret the verses of the Quran however they wish according to their own desires.

2. Why This Much Emphasis on the Resurrection?

We said that among the most important issues emphasized in the 30th part of the Holy Quran—the vast majority of whose Surahs are Makkan—is the issue of the "Resurrection" and the description of man's states on the Day of Rising.

This is because the first step in reforming a human being is for him to know that there is an accounting and a reckoning.

There exists a court where nothing is hidden from its judges.

A tribunal where neither injustice nor oppression finds a way, nor error nor mistake.

Neither recommendation nor bribery is effective there, nor is there the possibility of lying or denial. Ultimately, there is no way to escape the clutches of punishment there; the only way is to abandon sin here.

Faith in the existence of such a tribunal and court shakes a person, awakens sleeping souls, revives the spirit of piety, commitment, and sense of responsibility, and invites him to the recognition of duty.

In principle, in any environment where corruption penetrates, its cause is one of two things: weakness of the monitoring force, or weakness of the judicial system. If sharp-eyed monitors keep the actions of humans under surveillance, and courts precisely address the crimes of violators, and no "crime" remains without "penalty," then in such an environment, corruption, sin, transgression, encroachment, and rebellion will certainly reach a minimum.

Where material life is such under the light of its monitors and courts, the status of man's spiritual and divine life is clear. Faith in the existence of an Origin that is with him everywhere—"Not even the weight of an atom escapes Him" (Saba: 3)—and faith in the existence of a Resurrection where, according to "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it" (Az-Zalzalah: 7-8), not an atom's weight of good or bad work is consigned to oblivion, but rather stands before him there—such faith creates such piety in a person that it can be his guide on the path of goodness throughout his entire life.

[^1]: Risalat al-I'tiqad by Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Mu'min al-Shirazi (as cited in Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, p. 484).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 420, Hadith 9.

[^2]: Ibid., p. 419, Hadith 3.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِهَـٰدًا (٦)
Did We not make the earth a resting place? (6)
وَٱلْجِبَالَ أَوْتَادًا (٧)
and the mountains stakes? (7)
وَخَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا (٨)
and create you in pairs? (8)
وَجَعَلْنَا نَوْمَكُمْ سُبَاتًا (٩)
and make your sleep for rest? (9)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا (١٠)
and make the night a covering? (10)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلنَّهَارَ مَعَاشًا (١١)
and make the day for livelihood? (11)
وَبَنَيْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعًا شِدَادًا (١٢)
and build above you the seven mighty heavens? (12)
وَجَعَلْنَا سِرَاجًا وَهَّاجًا (١٣)
and make [the sun for] a radiant lamp? (13)
وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمُعْصِرَٰتِ مَآءً ثَجَّاجًا (١٤)
and send down water pouring from the rain-clouds, (14)
لِّنُخْرِجَ بِهِۦ حَبًّا وَنَبَاتًا (١٥)
that We may bring forth with it grains and plants, (15)
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ أَلْفَافًا (١٦)
and luxuriant gardens? (16)

Commentary

All are bewildered by You and obedient to Your command...

These verses are, in fact, a response to the questions posed by the deniers of the Resurrection and those who disputed this "Great News." In these verses, a portion of the wise system of this universe and the calculated blessings that play a highly effective role in human life are described. On one hand, they serve as clear evidence of Allah's power over all things, including the restoration of life to the dead. On the other hand, they point to the fact that this wise system cannot be futile or in vain; for if everything were to end with the conclusion of this material worldly life, it would certainly be a futile and purposeless design.

Thus, from two perspectives, this constitutes an argument for the issue of Resurrection: through the "Argument of Power" and the "Argument of Wisdom."

In these eleven verses, twelve important blessings are mentioned using expressions blended with grace and affection, combined with logical reasoning and the stimulation of emotions; for if intellectual arguments are not accompanied by feeling and spiritual vitality, their effectiveness is diminished.

First, starting with the earth, He says: "Have We not made the earth a cradle and a place of rest for you?" (أَ لَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ مِهاداً).

"Mihad," as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, means a prepared, smooth, and organized place. It is originally derived from "Mahd," meaning the place prepared for a child's rest (whether a cradle or a bed). A group of linguists and commentators have interpreted it as "Firash," meaning a "bed" that is smooth, soft, and comfortable.

The choice of this expression for the earth is very meaningful. On one hand, vast parts of the earth are so soft, smooth, and organized that man can easily build houses and establish farms and orchards upon it.

On the other hand, all his needs are hidden on the surface of the earth or in its depths in the form of raw materials and precious minerals.

Thirdly, it absorbs his waste materials, and the bodies of the dead, when buried in it, are quickly decomposed and disintegrated. Various microbes are destroyed due to a mysterious property that the hand of Creation has placed in the soil.

Fourthly, with its smooth and rapid movement, it revolves around the sun and rotates on its axis, creating night and day and the four seasons, which play a major role in human life.

Fifthly, it stores a large portion of the water that falls on its surface within itself and sends it forth in the form of springs and subterranean canals (qanats).

In short, in this peaceful bed, all means of comfort and tranquility for the children of this earth are prepared and ready. The importance of this blessing becomes more apparent when even a slight tremor or upheaval occurs in it.

And since the importance of mountains and their vital role might be forgotten in contrast to the softness of the flat lands, He adds in the next verse: "And [did We not make] the mountains as pegs?" (وَ الْجِبالَ أَوْتاداً).

Mountains, in addition to having massive roots deep within the earth where they are interconnected—acting like armor to protect the earth's crust against the pressure of internal molten materials and the gravitational pull of the moon that creates tides—are considered high walls against harsh and heavy storms. They create a secure shelter for man's cradle of rest; had they not existed, human life would have been constantly subjected to the unrest caused by the pounding blows of storms.

Furthermore, they are centers for storing water and various precious minerals.

In addition to all this, there is a massive layer of air around the globe which, due to the existence of mountains that grip this vast layer like the cogs of a wheel, moves along with the earth. Scientists say that if the earth's surface were smooth, the air layer would slide over it as the earth moved, creating massive storms, and this constant friction could make the earth's surface scorching hot and uninhabitable.

After mentioning these two examples of external blessings and signs (Afaq), He turns to the internal blessings of human existence and the signs within the self (Anfus) and says: "And We created you in pairs" (وَ خَلَقْناكُمْ أَزْواجاً) [1]. "Azwaj" is the plural of "Zawj," meaning a pair, and the "male and female" genders. The creation of man from these two genders, besides guaranteeing the survival of his race, is considered a cause for the tranquility of his body and soul, as we read in Surah Ar-Rum, verse 21:

وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَ رَحْمَةً: "And of His signs is that He created for you from yourselves mates that you may find tranquility in them; and He placed between you affection and mercy."

In other words, the male and female genders are each a complement to the other's existence and a remedy for the deficiencies of the opposite side.

And since "Azwaj" in the language also means "categories and types," some consider this verse to refer to the various categories of humans and the differences in color, race, temperaments, and various talents among people, which is also one of the signs of the Truth's greatness and a means for the perfection of human society.

Then He points to the phenomenon of "sleep," which is one of the great divine blessings upon man, adding: "And We made your sleep [a means for] rest" (وَ جَعَلْنا نَوْمَكُمْ سُباتاً).

[1]: The sentence "وَ خَلَقْناكُمْ أَزْواجاً" and similar sentences in the following verses are independent positive sentences. The possibility suggested by some that they are negative and conjoined to the concept of "أ لم نجعل" in the previous verse seems unlikely and would require an implied omission.

"Subat" is from the root "Sabt," which originally means to cut off, and subsequently came to mean "ceasing work" for the purpose of rest. The fact that "Saturday" is called "Yawm al-Sabt" in Arabic is because this naming was influenced by the practices of the Jews, who considered Saturday a day of rest.

The expression "Subat" is a subtle reference to the cessation of significant portions of man's physical and mental activities during sleep. This temporary cessation allows for rest, the restoration of worn-out organs, the strengthening of the soul and body, the renewal of human vitality, the removal of all fatigue and discomfort, and finally, readiness for renewed activity.

Despite the fact that "sleep" occupies one-third of human life and man has always faced this phenomenon, the secrets of sleep are still not fully understood. Even what factor causes a part of the brain's activities to cease at a specific moment, followed by the closing of the eyelids and all body members falling into stillness and silence, is still not clearly known! However, it is clear that "sleep" plays a massive role in human health. For this reason, psychiatrists strive to regulate the sleep of their patients normally, because without it, mental balance is impossible.

Individuals who do not sleep naturally are withered, irritable, depressed, sad, and uneasy. Conversely, those who enjoy moderate sleep feel extraordinary vitality and power when they wake up.

Studying after a refreshing sleep progresses very quickly, and intellectual and physical tasks after such sleep are always accompanied by success. All of these highlight the highly important role of sleep in human life.

Few tortures are as painful and agonizing for a human as "forced and involuntary insomnia." Experience has shown that human tolerance for lack of sleep is very low, and after a short period, one loses health and becomes ill.

Of course, what has been said about the importance of sleep refers to a balanced and appropriate sleep; otherwise, oversleeping, like overeating, is a negative trait and causes various diseases.

Surprisingly, the amount of natural sleep is not the same for all humans, and no specific limit can be set for it. Therefore, everyone must discover their own need for sleep through experience, considering the level of their physical and mental activities.

Even more surprising is that during severe events where a person is forced to stay awake for long periods, human resistance to insomnia temporarily increases; sleep flees from the person, sometimes reaching the minimum necessary—one or two hours. But it has often been seen that this deficit is compensated for when conditions return to normal, and the human body and soul reclaim their debt from sleep!

Of course, rarely are there individuals found who stay awake for months on end without a moment of sleep entering their eyes. Conversely, there are individuals who are overcome by sleep even while walking in the street or even while speaking to you; if someone is not with them, it is dangerous. But these individuals are certainly not healthy and will inevitably suffer physical and mental damage.

In short, this strange transformation and change called "sleep" that occurs in man has many wonders that make it resemble a "miracle." [1]

[1]: Regarding the wonders of the world of sleep, we have provided another discussion in Volume 16, page 404 onwards (under verse 24 of Surah Ar-Rum). Also, regarding the scenes man sees in sleep (dreams) and its wonders, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 9, pages 311 to 317 (under verse 4 of Surah Yusuf).

Although the above verse regards sleep as a divine blessing, since "sleep" bears a resemblance to "death" and "waking" bears a resemblance to "resurrection," it can also be an allusion to this matter.

Then, in connection with the issue of "sleep," He speaks of the blessing of "night," saying: "And We made the night as a clothing" (وَ جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِباساً).

And immediately He adds: "And We made the day for livelihood" (وَ جَعَلْنَا النَّهارَ مَعاشاً) [1]. Contrary to what the "Dualists" (fire-worshippers/Manichaeans) imagined due to their ignorance of the secrets of creation—thinking that the light of day is a blessing and the darkness of night is evil and a punishment, attributing a creator to each, one from "Yazdan" (God) and the other from "Ahriman" (Satan)—with a little reflection, it becomes clear that each is a great blessing in its place and a source of other blessings.

According to the above verses, the veil of night is a garment and a covering over the body of the earth, and all...

[1]: "Ma'ash" can be a noun of time or place meaning the time and place of living, or it can be a mim-infinitive, in which case there is an implied word, and the estimation is "a cause for your livelihood." Additionally, "Ma'ash" is from the root "Aysh" meaning life, and its difference from "Hayat" is that "Hayat" is also applied to Allah and the angels, while "Aysh" is specific to the life of humans and animals.

...living creatures that exist upon it are compelled to cease the exhausting activities of life. It allows darkness, which is the source of stillness, peace, and rest, to prevail over everything, so that worn-out limbs may be repaired and the weary soul may renew its vitality; for peaceful sleep is not possible except in darkness.

Furthermore, with the falling of the veil of night, the sunlight is withdrawn. If it were to shine continuously, it would burn all plants and animals, and the earth would not be a place for life! For this reason, the Holy Quran repeatedly emphasizes this issue. In one place, He says: قُلْ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ النَّهارَ سَرْمَداً إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ‌: "Say, 'Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the day continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you a night in which you may rest?'" (Al-Qasas: 72). And following that, He says:

وَ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌: "And out of His mercy He made for you the night and the day that you may rest therein and [by day] seek from His bounty" (Al-Qasas: 73).

It is noteworthy that in the Holy Quran, many important subjects are sworn by once, whereas "night" is sworn by seven times! We know that oaths are taken by important matters, and this itself is a sign of the importance of the veil of the night's darkness.

Those who illuminate the "night" with artificial light, staying awake all night and sleeping during the day instead, are sickly, unhealthy, and lack vitality. In villages, where unlike cities they sleep early at night and rise early in the morning, people live healthier lives.

Night also has secondary benefits, as its pre-dawn hours are the best time for intimate prayer to the Beloved, worship, self-building, and the education of souls, just as the Holy Quran says in describing the pious: وَ بِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ‌ "And in the hours before dawn they would ask forgiveness" (Adh-Dhariyat: 18) [1]. The light of day is also a peerless blessing; it creates hustle, bustle, and movement, prepares man for work and effort, makes plants grow in its light, and animals thrive under it. Truly, the above expression which says: "We made the day a means for your livelihood and life" is an eloquent expression in every respect that needs no further description.

The final point is that the alternation of night and day and the precise system of their gradual changes is one of the signs of creation and the signs of God. Moreover, it is considered the source of a natural calendar for the temporal organization of human lives.

Then, moving from the earth to the "sky," He says: "And We built above you seven strong [heavens]" (وَ بَنَيْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعاً شِداداً).

The number "seven" here might be a number of "multiplicity," referring to the numerous celestial bodies, the collections of solar systems, galaxies, and the multiple worlds of the universe, which possess a firm creation and a massive, strong structure. Or it could be a "literal count," such that all the stars we see—according to verse 6 of Surah As-Saffat: إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ‌ "Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven with an adornment of stars"—belong to the first heaven, and beyond it exist six other worlds and heavens that are beyond the reach of human science.

There is also the possibility that it refers to the multiple layers of air surrounding the earth, which, despite their apparent thinness, possess such strength that they protect this earthly globe from the constant onslaught of meteors. As soon as one of them is pulled toward the earth, it becomes so hot due to the intense collision with the air layer that it catches fire and burns, and its ash settles gently on the ground. If this air layer did not exist, our cities and settlements would be exposed to the bombardment of these stones day and night.

Some scientists have calculated that the resistance of the air layer around the earth, which is more than a hundred kilometers thick, is equivalent to a "steel roof ten meters thick!" And this is one of the interpretations of "Sab'an Shidada" [1].

After a brief reference to the creation of the heavens, He turns to the great blessing of the world-illuminating sun and says: "And We made a glowing, heat-giving lamp" (وَ جَعَلْنا سِراجاً وَهَّاجاً) [2]. "Wahhaj" is from the root "Wahaj," meaning the light and heat emitted from fire [3]. Therefore, mentioning this description for this brilliant celestial lamp...

[1]: Regarding the seven heavens and their interpretations, we have discussed them in detail in Volume 1 (under verse 29 of Surah Al-Baqarah). [2]: "Ja'alna" here means "Khalaqna" (We created) and takes one object. [3]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "Wahaj"; in Lisan al-Arab, it says the meaning of this word is the heat of the sun and fire from a distance.

...is an allusion to two great blessings that are the essence of all material favors in this world: "light" and "heat."

The sun's light not only illuminates the scene of human life and the entire solar system but also has a profound effect on the development of living beings. Its heat, in addition to its direct effect on the life of humans, animals, and plants, is the primary source for the existence of clouds, the blowing of winds, the falling of rain, and the irrigation of dry lands.

The sun, due to its specific "ultraviolet" rays, has a great effect on killing microbes. If it were not for this, the earth would turn into a massive hospital, and perhaps in a short time, the race of living beings would perish. The sun provides us all with a healthy, free, and permanent light from an appropriate distance—neither too hot and scorching nor cold and lifeless.

If we were to calculate the price of the energy obtained from the sun compared to other energy sources, it would constitute a massive figure. If, for instance, we wanted to grow an apple tree with artificial light and energy, the price of each single apple would be staggering. Yes, this "glowing lamp" of creation provides all of this to us for free [1].

[1]: In the book The World of Stars by Anne Terry White, a calculation is made regarding the light and heat the sun gives to the people of the earth. It says: If we were to pay for the light and heat we receive for free from the sun according to the usual price of "electricity," the people on earth would have to pay one billion seven hundred million dollars every hour, which amounts to a staggering sum in a year. This shows what a massive wealth reaches us for free through this means. And according to the author of the book From Distant Worlds, if the people of the earth wanted to receive the light they get from the sun from lamps installed in the same location where the sun is placed, for every single person, more than "five million billion hundred-watt lamps" would have to be lit!

The mass of the sun, which is about "one million three hundred thousand" times that of the earth, its distance of about "one hundred and fifty million" kilometers, the external surface temperature of the sun reaching six thousand degrees Celsius, and its internal temperature estimated at about twenty million degrees—all of it is so calculated that if it were slightly less or more, it would make life difficult for the inhabitants of the earth and make the continuation of life impossible, the explanation of which does not fit within this brief discourse.

Following the blessing of light and heat, He speaks of another important vital substance closely related to the sun's radiation, adding: "And We sent down from the rain-clouds pouring water" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ ماءً ثَجَّاجاً).

"Mu'sirat" is the plural of "Mu'sir" from the root "Asr" meaning pressure, referring to "rain-clouds," as if the cloud presses itself so that water falls from within it [1] (note that "Mu'sirat" is an active participle).

Some have also interpreted it as clouds that are ready to pour rain...

[1]: According to some scientists, when clouds condense, a system governs them that causes them to press themselves, resulting in rain falling from them. This expression is, in fact, considered one of the scientific miracles of the Holy Quran (refer to the book Wind and Rain, page 126).

...because the active participle sometimes denotes readiness for something. Some have also said "Mu'sirat" is not an adjective for the clouds but for the winds that pressure the clouds from every side to pour rain.

And "Thajjaj" is from the root "Thajj," meaning the pouring of water continuously and abundantly. Given that "Thajjaj" is an intensive form (Sighah al-Mubalaghah), it expresses even greater quantity and abundance. Overall, the meaning of the verse is that We sent down from the rain-clouds abundant and continuous water.

Although the falling of rain is a source of good and blessing in itself—it softens the air, washes away pollutants, carries away filth, lowers the heat of the air, and even moderates the cold, reduces disease factors, and gives man spirit and joy—nevertheless, in the following verses, He points to three of its important benefits, saying: "That We may bring forth thereby grain and vegetation" (لِنُخْرِجَ بِهِ حَبًّا وَ نَباتاً).

"And gardens of thick foliage" (وَ جَنَّاتٍ أَلْفافاً). "Alfafa," according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, refers to the fact that the trees of these gardens are so numerous and dense that they are entwined with one another [1].

[1]: "Alfafa," according to many linguists and commentators, is the plural of "Lafif" meaning "entwined." Some also consider it the plural of "Luff" (with a damma on the lam) and some the plural of "Liff" (with a kasra on the lam), and some say it is one of those plurals that has no singular, but the first is the most famous.

In fact, in these two verses, all the food substances used by humans and animals that grow from the earth are mentioned. For a major part of them consists of food grains (Habban), another part consists of vegetables and roots (wa Nabatan), and another part consists of fruits (wa Jannat).

It is true that in these two verses only these three great benefits of falling rain are mentioned, but undoubtedly the benefits of rain are not limited to these. Fundamentally, about seventy percent of the human body is composed of water, and the source of the emergence of all living beings, according to the explicit text of the Quran, is water: وَ جَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَيٍ‌ "And We made from water every living thing" (Al-Anbiya: 30). Therefore, water plays the primary and fundamental role regarding living beings, especially humans.

Not only the human body, but most factories would also be paralyzed without water, and the industrial system would collapse. The beauty of nature's face and its vitality are due to water, and the best commercial and economic roads in the world are the waterways.

Key Points The Connection of These Verses to the Issue of "Resurrection"

In the above eleven verses, the most important divine blessings and the most fundamental pillars of human life—namely "light," "darkness," "heat," "water," "soil," and "plants"—have been mentioned.

The description of this precise system is, on one hand, clear evidence of Allah's power over all things. Therefore, there remains no room for the question of how it is possible for Allah to once again return the dead to life, just as it is clearly stated in the response to the deniers of Resurrection in the final verses of Surah Ya-Sin: "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the likes of them?" (Ya-Sin: 81).

On the other hand, this massive organization must have a purpose, and this purpose certainly cannot be the few days of worldly life, limited to eating, drinking, sleeping, and waking. Rather, Allah's wisdom necessitates that there be a higher goal for it. In other words, the "first creation" is a reminder for the "hereafter" and a station for the long journey of mankind, as He says in verse 115 of Surah Al-Mu'minun: أَ فَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناكُمْ عَبَثاً وَ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنا لا تُرْجَعُونَ‌ "Then did you think that We created you uselessly and that to Us you would not be returned?"

Thirdly, the issue of sleep and waking, which is itself an example of death and renewed life, and the issue of the revival of dead lands through the falling of rain—which displays the scene of Resurrection before human eyes every year—are meaningful allusions to the issue of Resurrection and life after death, just as He says in verse 9 of Surah Fatir after mentioning the renewal of life in dead lands with the falling of rain: كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ: "Thus is the Resurrection."

Ayahs

إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ كَانَ مِيقَـٰتًا (١٧)
Indeed the Day of Judgement is the tryst, (17)
يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْوَاجًا (١٨)
the day the Trumpet will be blown, and you will come in groups, (18)
وَفُتِحَتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ أَبْوَٰبًا (١٩)
and the sky will be opened and become gates, (19)
وَسُيِّرَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ فَكَانَتْ سَرَابًا (٢٠)
and the mountains will be set moving and become a mirage. (20)

Commentary

Finally, the Promised Day Arrives

In the preceding verses, references were made to various proofs of the Resurrection. In the first verse under discussion, as a conclusion, it states: "Indeed, the Day of Sorting (the Day of Resurrection) is an appointed time for all" (إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ كانَ مِيقاتاً) [1]. The expression "Day of Sorting" (Yowm al-Fasl) is a very profound term that signifies the separations occurring on that Great Day:

[1]: Usually, the use of "kana" (was), which is a past tense verb, regarding that day which will be realized in the future, is to express the absolute certainty of that day.

The separation of Truth from Falsehood.

The separation of the ranks of the righteous believers from the wicked criminals.

The separation of father and mother from child, and brother from brother.

"Miqat" is derived from the root "waqt" (time), similar to "mi'ad" and "wa'd," meaning a specific and appointed time. The reason the specific locations where pilgrims of the House of Allah enter the state of Ihram are called "Miqat" is that they gather there at a specific time.

Then, describing some of the characteristics and events of that Great Day, it says: "The Day the Trumpet is blown, and you will come forth in crowds" (يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْواجاً). It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that two great events occur as the "Blowing of the Trumpet." In the first event, the order of the universe is disrupted, and all inhabitants of the earth and everyone in the heavens die. In the second event, the world is reconstructed, the dead return to a new life, and the Great Resurrection takes place.

"Nafkh" means "blowing" and "Sur" means "trumpet" or "horn," which is usually sounded to signal a caravan or army to halt, or to begin moving. The members of the caravan and the soldiers understand from the different tones of these two sounds whether they must stop or move.

This expression is a subtle and beautiful metaphor for those two great events, and what is mentioned in the above verse refers to the "Second Blowing of the Trumpet," which is the "Trumpet of Life, Rebirth, and Resurrection."

(Regarding the Blowing of the Trumpet and related points, we have discussed them in detail in Volume 19, under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar, pages 534 to 542). The verse under discussion says: On that day, you will enter the gathering place in crowds, while verse 95 of Surah Maryam says:

"And every one of them will come to Him on the Day of Resurrection alone" (وَ كُلُّهُمْ آتِيهِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَرْداً). And verse 71 of Surah Al-Isra says: "The Day We shall call every people with their Imam (leader)" (يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ).

The reconciliation between these verses is that people being in crowds does not contradict each crowd entering the gathering with its leader. As for them being "alone," it is because the Resurrection has multiple stages (stations). It is possible that in the initial stages, people enter the gathering in groups with their leaders of guidance or misguidance, but when standing before the Court of Divine Justice, they are individuals. As the Quran expresses in verse 21 of Surah Qaf, each person arrives there with a driver and a witness: "And every soul will come, with it a driver and a witness" (وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ).

There is also the possibility that being "alone" refers to being separated from friends, supporters, and helpers, for there, man is left with only himself and his deeds.

Following that, it adds: "And the heaven is opened and becomes as gates" (وَ فُتِحَتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ أَبْواباً). What is meant by these "gates"? And what does "being opened" signify?

Some have said: It means that the gates of the "Unseen" (Ghayb) world are opened to the "Manifest" (Shahadah) world, veils are removed, and the world of angels finds a path to the world of humans [1].

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 265.

However, a group has considered this verse a reference to what is mentioned in other verses of the Quran, which state: "When the heaven is split asunder" (إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ - Al-Inshiqaq: 1), and elsewhere expressed the same meaning with another term: "When the heaven is cleft asunder" (إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ - Al-Infitar: 1).

In reality, so many cracks appear in the celestial spheres that it is as if the entire heaven has turned into gates.

There is also the possibility that under current worldly conditions, man is unable to travel through the heavens, and if it is possible for him, it is very limited; it is as if current conditions have closed all the gates of heaven to him. But on the Day of Resurrection, man is freed from the earthly sphere, and the gates for traveling to the heavens are opened to him, and the conditions for it are provided.

In other words: On that day, first the heavens are disintegrated, and following that, according to "Verse 48 of Surah Ibrahim," new heavens and a new earth take their place:

"The Day the earth will be replaced by another earth, and the heavens [as well]" (يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَ السَّماواتُ). In this state, the gates of the heavens are opened to the inhabitants of the earth, and the path to the heavens is opened to man. The people of Paradise will go toward Paradise, and the gates of Paradise will be opened to them: "Until, when they reach it while its gates have been opened and its keepers say to them, 'Peace be upon you...'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَ فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها وَ قالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ) (Az-Zumar: 73).

And it is here that the angels enter upon them from every gate, offering congratulations and greetings: "And the angels will enter upon them from every gate" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ) (Ar-Ra'd: 23).

And the gates of Hell will also be opened to the disbelievers: "And those who disbelieved will be driven to Hell in groups until, when they reach it, its gates are opened" (وَ سِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها) (Az-Zumar: 71).

Thus, man steps into an arena whose width is as the width of the current heavens and earth: "And a Paradise whose width is the heavens and earth" (وَ جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ) (Al-Imran: 133).

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, reflecting the state of the mountains on the Day of Resurrection, it says: "And the mountains are moved and become as a mirage" (وَ سُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً). As gathered from the synthesis of various Quranic verses regarding the "fate of the mountains on the Day of Resurrection," the mountains go through several stages. First, the mountains are set in "motion": "And the mountains will move with a [terrible] motion" (وَ تَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً) (At-Tur: 10).

Then they are uprooted and violently crushed: "And the earth and the mountains are lifted and leveled with a single blow" (وَ حُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً) (Al-Haqqah: 14).

Afterward, they become like "a heap of pouring sand": "And the mountains become a heap of sand pouring down" (وَ كانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلًا) (Al-Muzzammil: 14).

Then they become like "carded wool" that moves with a strong wind: "And the mountains will be like wool, fluffed up" (وَ تَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ) (Al-Qari'ah: 5).

And then they become like "dust" scattered in the air: "And the mountains are crumbled to powder, becoming scattered dust" (وَ بُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا) (Al-Waqi'ah: 5-6).

And finally, as stated in the verse under discussion, only a trace of them remains, appearing from afar like a "mirage."

In this way, the mountains are eventually removed from the face of the earth, and the earth becomes level: "And they ask you about the mountains, so say, 'My Lord will blast them into particles. And He will leave the earth a level plain'" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالِ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً فَيَذَرُها قاعاً صَفْصَفاً) (Taha: 105-106).

"Sarab" (mirage) is from the root "sarab" (rhyming with 'taraf'), meaning to walk on a downward slope. Since in deserts during hot weather, when a person moves on a slope, a shimmer appears from afar that they mistake for water—while it is nothing but the "refraction of light"—subsequently, anything that has an appearance but no reality is called a "sarab." Thus, the above verse describes the beginning of this movement and its end, while the other stages are mentioned in other verses.

In reality, the mountains, in the form of dust in the air, take on a mirage-like state. When a mountain, with all its grandeur and solidity, meets such a fate, it is clear what parallel transformations occur in the world. Likewise, individuals or powers that had a mountain-like appearance in the life of this world will be nothing more than a mirage there! Here, the question arises: do these events occur during the "First Blowing," which pertains to the end of the world? Or during the "Second Blowing," which is the beginning of the Resurrection?

However, considering that the verse "The Day the Trumpet is blown, and you will come forth in crowds" certainly pertains to the "Second Blowing" when humans are resurrected and enter the gathering in crowds, this verse should also normally pertain to the same blowing. However, it is possible that the beginning of this (the movement of mountains) occurs in the "First Blowing," and its end (becoming a mirage) occurs in the "Second Blowing."

There is also the possibility that all stages of the disintegration of mountains pertain to the First Blowing; however, because there is not a long interval between the two, they are mentioned together, just as in some other Quranic verses, the events of the First and Second Blowings are mentioned together (examples of this can be seen in Surah At-Takwir and Al-Infitar—observe closely).

It is noteworthy that in previous verses, the mountains were introduced as "pegs" and the earth as a "cradle." In the verses under discussion, it says that on the day the command for the world's annihilation is issued, that cradle will be shaken, and these massive pegs will be uprooted. It is clear that when the pegs of something are pulled out, it disintegrates.

Ayahs

إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كَانَتْ مِرْصَادًا (٢١)
Indeed hell is in ambush, (21)
لِّلطَّـٰغِينَ مَـَٔابًا (٢٢)
a resort for the rebels, (22)
لَّـٰبِثِينَ فِيهَآ أَحْقَابًا (٢٣)
to reside therein for ages, (23)
لَّا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا بَرْدًا وَلَا شَرَابًا (٢٤)
tasting in it neither any coolness nor drink, (24)
إِلَّا حَمِيمًا وَغَسَّاقًا (٢٥)
except boiling water and pus, (25)
جَزَآءً وِفَاقًا (٢٦)
a fitting requital. (26)
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ حِسَابًا (٢٧)
Indeed they did not expect any reckoning, (27)
وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كِذَّابًا (٢٨)
and they denied Our signs mendaciously, (28)
وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ كِتَـٰبًا (٢٩)
and We have figured everything in a Book. (29)
فَذُوقُوا۟ فَلَن نَّزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذَابًا (٣٠)
So [now] taste! We shall increase you in nothing but punishment! (30)

Commentary

Hell, the Great Ambush!

After presenting some proofs for the Resurrection and describing a portion of the events of the Day of Judgment, the discourse turns toward the fate of the inhabitants of Hell and Paradise. Starting first with the "denizens of Hell," it states: "Indeed, Hell has been lying in wait" (إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كانَتْ مِرْصاداً).

"And a place of return for the transgressors!" (لِلطَّاغِينَ مَآباً) [1]

"They will remain therein for long ages" (لابِثِينَ فِيها أَحْقاباً). "Mirsad" is a noun of place meaning a location where one lies in wait. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Marsad" and "Mirsad" both carry the same meaning, with the difference that "Mirsad" refers to a place specifically designated for ambushing.

[1] The verse has an omitted part, and the implied structure is: kanat lil-taghina ma’aba.

Some have also stated it is an "intensive form" (sighah al-mubalaghah), meaning one who lies in wait extensively, similar to "mi'mar," which refers to a person who builds and develops extensively.

Of course, the first meaning is both more famous and more appropriate. Regarding who is lying in wait for the transgressors in Hell, it is said: the angels of punishment. This is because, according to Verse 71 of Surah Maryam, all human beings—righteous and wicked alike—shall pass by Hell or over it: "And there is none of you but he shall pass over it; this is a fixed decree of your Lord" (وَ إِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها كانَ عَلى‌ رَبِّكَ حَتْماً مَقْضِيًّا). In this universal passage, the angels of punishment lie in wait and seize the denizens of Hell! If we interpret it as an "intensive form," then Hell itself lies in wait for them; as each transgressor approaches, it pulls them toward itself and swallows them into its maw. In any case, no rebel can cross this public passage; either the angels of punishment seize them, or the intense gravity of Hell pulls them in.

"Ma'ab" means a place of return or destination, and sometimes it refers to an abode or dwelling place, which is the meaning intended here.

As for "Ahqab," it is the plural of "huqb," meaning an indefinite period of time. Some have interpreted it as eighty years, some seventy, and some forty years. Since this expression implies that the inhabitants of Hell stay there for long periods which eventually end—and this seems to contradict the verses regarding "Khulud" (eternity) and permanent punishment—commentators have sought various explanations.

The well-known view among commentators is that "Ahqab" here refers to long periods and many years that follow one another continuously without end; as one period passes, another takes its place.

In some narrations, it is also mentioned that this verse pertains to sinners who are eventually purified and released from Hell, not the disbelievers who dwell in the Fire eternally [1].

Then, pointing to a small corner of the great punishments of Hell, it adds: "They will taste therein neither coolness to quench the terrifying heat of Hell, nor any pleasant drink to soothe their intense thirst" (لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيها بَرْداً وَ لا شَراباً).

"Except boiling water and a fluid of pus and blood" (إِلَّا حَمِيماً وَ غَسَّاقاً). And except the shadow of thick, hot, and suffocating smoke of the Fire mentioned in Verse 43 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah: "And a shadow of black smoke" (وَ ظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ).

"Hamim" means extremely hot water, and "Ghassaq" means the pus and blood that flows from a wound; some have interpreted it as foul-smelling liquids.

This is while the inhabitants of Paradise are quenched from the pleasant springs of pure wine by their Lord: "And their Lord will give them a pure drink" (وَ سَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَراباً طَهُوراً) (Al-Insan: 21), and drinks in beautiful heavenly vessels that are sealed—a seal made of musk: "The seal thereof is musk" (خِتامُهُ مِسْكٌ) (Al-Mutaffifin: 26). See how vast the difference is between the two paths!

Since the existence of such severe and intense punishments might seem strange to some, the next verse adds: "This is a recompense fitting and appropriate to their deeds"

[1] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 494, Hadith 23, and p. 495, Hadith 26.

(جَزاءً وِفاقاً) [1]. Why should it not be so? While in this world, they burned the hearts of the oppressed, set fire to their souls and lives, and showed mercy to no one through their tyranny and rebellion, it is fitting that their punishment there be such, and their drinks be such! Fundamentally, as we have emphasized many times, it is understood from the Quranic verses that at least many of the punishments of the Resurrection are the embodiment of human deeds in this world, as we read in Surah Al-Tahrim, Verse 7: "O you who disbelieve! Make no excuses this Day! You are being recompensed only for what you used to do" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ)—meaning your deeds have now taken form and are present before you.

Then, explaining the reason for this punishment, He says: "This is because they did not expect any reckoning, nor did they fear any retribution from Allah" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا لا يَرْجُونَ حِساباً). And this very indifference to the reckoning and the Day of Judgment became the source of their rebellion, defiance, and tyranny, and that oppression and corruption prepared such a painful fate for them. In truth, lack of belief in the Reckoning is a factor for rebellion, which in turn is a factor for those severe punishments.

Note that "la yarjun" from the root "raja'" means both "hope"

[1] "Jaza'an" is in the accusative case as a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq) for an omitted verb understood from the context, and "wifaqan" also carries a gerundial meaning and serves as its attribute. The implied structure is: yajzihim jaza'an dha wifaq (He recompenses them with a fitting recompense).

and "lack of fear and dread." Fundamentally, when a person expects and anticipates punishment, they naturally fear it; if they do not, they do not fear. These two are inseparable. Therefore, those who have no hope (expectation) of a reckoning have no fear either.

The use of "inna" for emphasis, "kanu" to express continuity in the past, and "hisaban" as an indefinite noun after a negation to imply generality—all these illustrate the reality that they absolutely and never had any expectation or fear of a reckoning. In other words, they had consigned the reckoning of the Day of Resurrection to oblivion and completely removed it from their life's program. It is natural for such individuals to become tainted with such great sins and ultimately be caught in these severe and painful punishments.

Therefore, He immediately adds: "And they utterly denied Our signs" (وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا كِذَّاباً) [1]. Their carnal desires had so overcome them that they vehemently denied all of Allah's awakening signs so they could continue their rebellious whims and fulfill their illegitimate desires. It is clear that "signs" (ayat) here has a broad meaning, encompassing all signs of Monotheism, Prophethood, legislation, and creation, as well as the miracles of the Prophets and the laws and traditions. Given the denial of all these divine signs that fill the worlds of creation and legislation, we must concede that such punishment for such people is a "fitting recompense" (jaza'an wifaqan).

Then, as a warning to these transgressors and to emphasize the balance between "crime" and "penalty" and the sovereignty of "fitting recompense," He adds: "And everything We have certainly enumerated and recorded" (وَ كُذَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ كِتاباً) [1], so that you do not imagine any of your deeds will go without reckoning or punishment, nor should you ever think these severe punishments are unjust. This reality—that all human actions, small or large, minor or major, manifest or hidden, and even human intentions and beliefs, are recorded and preserved—is reflected in many verses of the Quran.

In one place He says: "And everything they did is in the records. And every small and great thing is inscribed" (وَ كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِي الزُّبُرِ وَ كُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَ كَبِيرٍ مُسْتَطَرٌ) (Al-Qamar: 52-53). And in another place: "Indeed, Our messengers record what you plot" (إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ) (Yunus: 21). And elsewhere we read: "We record that which they send before them and their footprints" (وَ نَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَ آثارَهُمْ) (Ya-Sin: 12).

[1] "Kidhaban" (with a kasra on the kaf) is one of the forms of the verbal noun for the taf'il pattern, meaning "denial." Some linguists have also said it is a verbal noun of the simple triliteral verb equivalent to "kadhib." In any case, it is a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq) for "kadhdhabu" used for emphasis.

[1] In this verse, "kulla" is in the accusative case due to a hidden verb indicated by the mentioned verb "ahsaynahu," and "kitaban" is in the accusative as a "cognate accusative" because "ahsayna" carries the meaning of "katabna" (We wrote); some have taken it as a circumstantial qualifier (hal).

Therefore, when the record of deeds is handed to the criminals, their cry rises; they say: "Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing small or great, but has enumerated it!" (يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها) (Al-Kahf: 49). Undoubtedly, one who believes this reality with all their heart will be very precise and calculating in their actions. This belief creates a great barrier between man and sin and is considered one of the important factors of spiritual training.

In the final verse under discussion, the tone of the speech changes from "third person" to "direct address," speaking to them directly. Within a threatening, wrathful, and soul-shaking sentence, He says: "So taste! For We shall never increase you in anything except punishment!" (فَذُوقُوا فَلَنْ نَزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذاباً). No matter how much you cry out, say "Woe to us," or request to return to the world to compensate for your sins, it will not be heard from you, and nothing will be added to you except punishment.

This is the recompense for those who, when faced with the loving invitation of the Prophets of Allah toward faith and piety, would say: "It is all the same to us whether you advise us or be not of those who advise!" (سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَ وَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ) (Al-Shu'ara: 136). And this is the recompense for one who, when the divine verses were recited to him, "it only increased them in aversion" (وَ ما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً) (Al-Isra: 41). And finally, this is the penalty for one who turned away from no sin and had no interest in any good deed.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said: "This verse is the most severe in the Quran regarding the people of the Fire." [1] How could it not be so, when the All-Forgiving and Most Merciful Allah has become so angry with them that He addresses them with a sentence that closes all doors of hope and gives them nothing but the promise of increasing punishment?

[1] Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 690. This hadith is also mentioned in Tafsir al-Safi under the verse in question, as well as in Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 307.

Ayahs

إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفَازًا (٣١)
Indeed a triumph awaits the Godwary: (31)
حَدَآئِقَ وَأَعْنَـٰبًا (٣٢)
gardens and vineyards, (32)
وَكَوَاعِبَ أَتْرَابًا (٣٣)
and buxom maidens of a like age, (33)
وَكَأْسًا دِهَاقًا (٣٤)
and brimming cups. (34)
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا كِذَّٰبًا (٣٥)
Therein they shall hear neither vain talk nor lies (35)
جَزَآءً مِّن رَّبِّكَ عَطَآءً حِسَابًا (٣٦)
—a reward and a sufficing bounty from your Lord, (36)
رَّبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا (٣٧)
the All-beneficent, the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. They will not be able to address Him (37)

Commentary

A Portion of the Great Reward for the Righteous

In the preceding verses, the discourse concerned the fate of the transgressors, a portion of their painful punishments, and the cause of this wretchedness. In the verses under discussion, the focus shifts to their opposite point, speaking of the true believers, the righteous, and a portion of their blessings in the Resurrection, so that through a face-to-face comparison between the two, the truths may become clearer. This is the method of the Holy Quran in other Surahs, where it places opposites against one another and explains their condition through this contrast.

First, It says: "Indeed, for the righteous is a great victory and attainment" (إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفازاً).

"Mafaz" is a "noun of place" or a "mim-infinitive" from the root "fawz," meaning "attaining goodness and virtue accompanied by safety." It also carries the meaning of salvation and victory, which is a necessary consequence of this meaning. Given that "mafaza" is mentioned in the indefinite form, it refers to a magnificent victory and the attainment of great goodness and felicity.

Then, describing this success and happiness, It adds: "Lush, green, and protected gardens and vineyards" (حَدائِقَ وَ أَعْناباً) [1]. "Hada'iq" is the plural of "hadiqah," meaning a lush, green garden or orchard full of trees that is enclosed by a wall and protected from all sides. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Hadiqah" originally refers to land that possesses water.

[1]: "Hada'iq" is a "substitute" (badal) for "mafaza" or its "explanatory apposition" (atf-e bayan).

Just like the "hadaqah" (pupil) of the eye, which always contains water! It is interesting that among all fruits, grapes are emphasized here because of the extraordinary advantages this fruit possesses among others. According to nutritionists, "grapes," in addition to being considered a complete food in terms of properties—with nutritional content very close to mother's milk—produce twice as much heat in the body as meat. Furthermore, they contain so many beneficial substances that they can be called a natural pharmacy.

"Grapes" possess antitoxic properties, are useful for purifying the blood, repelling rheumatism and gout, and act as an agent against increased blood urea.

In addition to these, grapes strengthen the nerves and produce joy, and due to containing various vitamins, they grant man power and strength.

These are a few of the effects and properties of grapes; hence, we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The best of your fruits is the grape."

Then, referring to the heavenly spouses, which is another of the blessings for the righteous, It adds: "For them are young maidens of equal age" (وَ كَواعِبَ أَتْراباً). "Kawa'ib" is the plural of "ka'ib," meaning a maiden whose youth is just blossoming, referring to the beginning of youth. "Atrab" is the plural of "tirb," meaning individuals of the same age, and it is mostly used for the female gender. Some say it is originally derived from "tara'ib," meaning the ribs of the chest, which bear a great resemblance to one another.

This being of equal age may be among the heavenly women themselves—meaning they are all young and equal in beauty, grace, and proportion of stature—or it may be between them and their spouses, as age compatibility between two spouses allows them to better understand each other's feelings. However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

Then, It describes the fourth heavenly blessing awaiting the righteous: "And cups full, overflowing, and successive of pure wine!" (وَ كَأْساً دِهاقاً). But not a wine like the contaminated wines of this world that strip away the intellect and degrade man to the level of an animal; rather, a wine that brings wisdom, creates joy, nourishes the soul, and uplifts the spirit.

"Ka's" refers to a cup filled with a drink, and sometimes it is applied to the cup itself or its contents.

Many commentators and linguists have interpreted "dihaq" to mean "overflowing," but Ibn Manzur in Lisan al-Arab has cited two other meanings for it: "successive" and "clear and pure."

Therefore, if we consider the sum of these meanings, the concept of the verse becomes that for the people of Paradise, cups filled with pure, clear, overflowing wine are brought successively.

Since the mention of cups and wine might evoke undesirable connotations from this world—whereas heavenly wine is the exact opposite of the satanic wines of this world—It immediately adds: "They will hear therein no ill speech or falsehood" (لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَ لا كِذَّاباً).

The wine of this world strips away the intellect, removes consciousness, and compels man toward vain talk and discordant speech; however, the pure heavenly wine grants man spirit, intellect, light, and clarity.

Regarding what the pronoun in "fiha" (therein) refers to, two possibilities have been given: first, that it returns to "Paradise," and second, that it returns to "ka's" (the cup).

According to the first interpretation, the meaning of the verse would be that in Paradise they hear no vain talk or lies, similar to what appears in verses 10 and 11 of Surah Al-Ghashiyah: فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ لا تَسْمَعُ فِيها لاغِيَةً ("In a high garden, wherein you will hear no unsuitable speech"). According to the second interpretation, the meaning is that from drinking that cup of wine, no vain talk or falsehood results, similar to what appears in verse 23 of At-Tur: يَتَنازَعُونَ فِيها كَأْساً لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَ لا تَأْثِيمٌ ("They will pass among themselves a cup [of wine] wherein there is no ill speech or commission of sin").

In any case, one of the great spiritual blessings for the people of Paradise is that there will be no trace of fabrications, vain talk, slanders, denials of truth, justifications of falsehood, or unpleasant conversations that hurt the hearts of the righteous in this world. Truly, how beautiful is that environment where no trace of these discordant and painful words exists, and according to verse 62 of Maryam: "They will not hear therein any ill speech—only [greetings of] peace" (لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً إِلَّا سَلاماً).

At the conclusion of mentioning these blessings, It refers to another spiritual blessing that is higher than all: "As a reward from your Lord, a sufficient gift" (جَزاءً مِنْ رَبِّكَ عَطاءً حِساباً) [1]. What glad tidings and blessing could be greater than this: that a weak servant is favored with the kindness, grace, and love of his Generous Master, who honors him, holds him in high esteem, and grants him a robe of honor? This attention and care, and this grace and love, grant such pleasure to the believers that no other blessing can equal it.

As the poet says: "Who am I to cross that fragrant mind? You bestow kindnesses, O You whose threshold's dust is the crown on my head."

The expression "your Lord" (ربّك) combined with the second-person pronoun and the word "gift" (عطاء) all signify the extraordinary grace hidden within these rewards.

The word "hisaba" (account/sufficient), according to many commentators, means "sufficient" (kafiya) here, as it is sometimes said "ahsabtu," meaning "I gave him so much that he said 'hasbi' (it is enough for me)" [2]. In a Hadith from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, it is mentioned that on the Day of Resurrection, Allah will account for the good deeds of the believers and reward each good deed tenfold up to seven hundredfold, just as Allah has said in the Quran: جَزاءً مِنْ رَبِّكَ عَطاءً حِساباً [3].

[1]: "Jaza'an" in this verse is a "circumstantial adverb" (hal) for the blessings mentioned in the previous verses, meaning: He gave them all of that as a reward from your Lord. Some have also suggested it is an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) for a hidden verb, and some have considered it a "purpose object" (maf'ul li-ajlih), but the first interpretation is more appropriate.

[2]: Tafsir al-Baydawi, under the verse discussed.

[3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 495, Hadith 29.

From this narration, it is understood that although Divine gifts are aspects of His favor, they are simultaneously based on the "account" of deeds; that is, His favors and gifts are proportionate to the righteous deeds of humans. In this way, "hisaba" in the above verse can be interpreted in its well-known meaning of "calculation," and there is no obstacle to combining this meaning with the previous one (reflect on this).

Then, in the final verse under discussion, It adds: "These great gifts are bestowed by the One who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the Most Merciful" (رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُمَا الرَّحْمنِ). Yes, He who is the Owner of this magnificent universe and its Sustainer and Educator, and whose mercy encompasses everything and everyone, is the same One who bestows all those gifts in the Resurrection upon the good and the pure.

In truth, the above verse is an allusion to the reality that if Allah gives such promises to the righteous, He has shown a portion of it in this world in the form of His general mercy to the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth.

At the end of the verse, It says: "And no one has the right to speak in His presence or intercede without His permission" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطاباً).

The pronoun in "la yamlikun" (they do not possess) may refer to all the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, or all the righteous and the transgressors gathered in the arena of Resurrection for accounting and reward. In any case, it indicates that no one on that Day has the right to object or question, for the Divine accounting is so precise and just that no room remains for "why or how."

Furthermore, no one has the right to intercede except by His leave and permission: "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Al-Baqarah: 255).

Key Points

1- The Gifts for the Righteous and the Punishments for the Transgressors

In the above verses, which speak of the blessings of the righteous, a striking contrast is seen when compared to the previous verses discussing the punishments of the transgressors.

Here, the talk is of "mafaz" (place of salvation), while there it was of "mirsad" (place of ambush).

Here, the talk is of fruit-filled gardens and "hada'iq and grapes," while there it was of being submerged in fire for an unlimited duration of "ahqab" (ages).

Here, the talk is of overflowing and successive cups of "pure wine," while there it was of scalding water and "hamim and ghassaq" (boiling fluid and pus).

Here, the talk is of the vast gifts of the "Most Merciful" (Rahman), while there it was of a just punishment and "jaza'an wifaqa" (an appropriate recompense).

Here, the talk is of the increase of Divine "blessing," while there it was of the increase of "torment."

In short, these two groups are at two opposite poles in every respect, because they were also at two opposite poles in terms of faith and action.

2- Heavenly Wines!

In various verses of the Holy Quran, many descriptions of heavenly wines are given, which, upon examination, show that their drinkers are immersed in such spiritual pleasure that it cannot be described by any words.

In one place, It describes it as "pure wine": "And their Lord will give them a pure drink" (Al-Insan: 21).

In another place, It emphasizes that this clear, unadulterated, and delightful wine creates neither headache, nor intoxication, nor corruption of the intellect: "There will be circulated among them a cup [of wine] from a flowing spring, white and delicious to the drinkers; no bad effect is there in it, nor from it will they be intoxicated" (As-Saffat: 45-47).

In one place, It says: They drink from a cup whose mixture is "camphor" (cool and calming): "Indeed, the righteous will drink from a cup [of wine] whose mixture is camphor" (Al-Insan: 5).

In another place, It adds: They are given to drink from a cup whose mixture is "ginger" (warming and invigorating): "And they will be given to drink a cup [of wine] whose mixture is ginger" (Al-Insan: 17).

In the verses under discussion, we also read: They have cups overflowing, clear, and successive (وَ كَأْساً دِهاقاً).

Most importantly, the "Saki" (cup-bearer) of this spiritual banquet is Allah!! They receive and drink from the hand of His power and from the spread of His grace and mercy, becoming intoxicated with the attraction of His love and gnosis: "And their Lord will give them a drink..." (Al-Insan: 21).

O Allah, grant us also from that pure wine.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلرُّوحُ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ صَفًّا ۖ لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا (٣٨)
on the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array: none shall speak except someone who is permitted by the All-beneficent and says what is rightful. (38)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ مَـَٔابًا (٣٩)
That day is true for certain. So let anyone who wishes take resort with his Lord. (39)
إِنَّآ أَنذَرْنَـٰكُمْ عَذَابًا قَرِيبًا يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ ٱلْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْكَافِرُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ تُرَٰبًۢا (٤٠)
Indeed We have warned you of a punishment near at hand—the day when a person will observe what his hands have sent ahead and the faithless one will say, ‘I wish I were dust!’ (40)

Commentary

The Disbelievers Say, "Would That I Were Dust!"

In the preceding verses, significant portions of the punishments and penalties for the transgressors, as well as the blessings and rewards for the righteous on the Day of Resurrection, were described.

The verses under discussion proceed to introduce that Great Day, explaining some of its attributes and events. It says:

"All of this occurs on the day when the Spirit and all the angels stand forth in ranks; they shall not speak except him whom the Rahman (the All-Merciful) permits, and he who speaks shall say only what is correct and right" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَ قالَ صَواباً) [1]. Undoubtedly, the standing of the "Spirit" and the "angels" on that day in a single rank, and their silence except by the permission of Allah the All-Merciful, is solely for the execution of His command. In this world, they are the "regulators of affairs" (mudabbirat al-amr) and the executors of His decrees, and in the Hereafter, this matter will be even more manifest, clear, and extensive.

Regarding what is meant by "the Spirit" (al-Ruh) here, commentators have mentioned many interpretations, reaching up to eight possibilities in some commentaries [2], the most important of which are:

  1. It refers to a creature other than the angels and superior to all of them.
  2. It refers to "Gabriel" (Jibra'il), the trustworthy messenger of Allah's revelation and the intermediary between Him and the Prophets, who is the greatest of the angels.
  3. It refers to the "souls of humans" who stand forth alongside the angels.
  4. It is an angel higher than all other angels and superior to Gabriel, who was and is accompanied by the Prophets and the Infallible Imams (علیهم السلام).

[^1]: Many commentators believe that "Day" (yawm) in this verse is an adverbial noun (zarf) related to the verb "they possess not" (la yamlikun) in the previous verse. It is also possible that it relates to everything mentioned in the preceding verses, as if it were: "All of that shall be on the day when the Spirit stands..." [^2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6977.

It is noteworthy that in the Holy Quran, "the Spirit" is sometimes mentioned in an absolute sense without any qualification, and in such cases, it is often placed in contrast to "the angels," such as:

"The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him..." (Ma'arij: 4) (تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ‌).
