"The angels and the Spirit descend therein by their Lord's leave with every decree" (Qadr: 4) (تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ).

In these two verses, "the Spirit" is mentioned after "the angels," while in the verse under discussion, it is mentioned before "the angels." Of course, this separation might be due to its status as a distinct and great individual—what is termed as mentioning the "general" after the "specific" or the "specific" after the "general."

However, in many verses, "the Spirit" appears with an addition or a description, such as "the Holy Spirit" (Ruh al-Qudus) in the verse: "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord in truth" (Nahl: 102) (قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِ‌), or the verse: "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down" (Shu'ara: 193) (نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ‌).

In some verses, Allah attributes "the Spirit" to Himself, saying:

"And I breathed into him of My Spirit" (Hijr: 29) (وَ نَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي‌)—a noble spirit that is attributed to His Sacred Essence due to its nobility. Elsewhere He says: "Then We sent to her Our Spirit" (Maryam: 17) (فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا).

It appears that "the Spirit" in these verses, expressed in different forms, may have different meanings, the explanation of each being provided under those respective verses.

However, what seems most appropriate among the various interpretations regarding the verse under discussion is that "the Spirit" here refers to one of the great divine angels who, according to some narrations, is even superior to Gabriel. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "He is an angel greater than Gabriel and Michael" [1]. In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is also stated: "The Spirit is an angel greater than Gabriel and Michael; he was with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) and he is with the Imams (علیهم السلام)" [2]. Although in some narrations in Sunni commentaries, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said: "The Spirit is an army from the armies of Allah; they are not angels. They have heads, hands, and feet." Then he recited: "The day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks," and added: "These are one army and those are another army" [3]. Regarding the human soul and its immateriality and independence, we have provided detailed discussions in Volume 12, pages 250 to 269 (under verse 85 of Surah al-Isra).

In any case, as mentioned, whether this great divine creature is from the angels or another being, on the Day of Resurrection, they stand alongside the angels ready to obey His command. Such awe and anxiety of the Gathering will have gripped everyone that no one will have the power to speak. And when they do utter a remembrance or a word, or perform intercession, it will only be by the permission of the Lord; they will speak His praise and glory or intercede for those worthy of intercession.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) was asked about this verse, and he said: "By Allah, we are those who are permitted on the Day of Resurrection and those who speak." The narrator asked, "What will you say on that day?" He replied: "We shall glorify our Lord, send blessings upon our Prophet, and intercede for our followers (Shi'a), and our Lord will not reject us" [1] [2]. From this narration, it is understood that the Prophets and the Infallible Imams also stand in the rank of the Spirit and the angels, and they are among those given permission to speak, praise Allah, and intercede.

The expression "correctly" (sawaban) is evidence that if the angels, the Spirit, or the Prophets and Saints intercede for anyone, it is based on a calculation and is not without reason.

Then, referring to this Great Day—which is the day of the standing of humans and angels, the Day of Sorting (Yawm al-Fasl), the day of punishment for the transgressors, and reward for the righteous—He says: "That is the True Day" (ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الْحَقُ‌). "Haqq" (True/Right) means something that is fixed, real, and will certainly happen, and this meaning is perfectly applicable to the Resurrection. Furthermore, it is the day when everyone's "right" is given to them, the rights of the oppressed are taken from the oppressors, and the "realities" and inner secrets are made manifest. Therefore, it is a day that is "True" in every sense of the word.

And since awareness of this reality can be the most effective motivation for a human to move toward his Lord and obey His command, He immediately adds: "So let him who will, take a way of return to his Lord" (فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ مَآباً).

This means that all the means for this divine movement are provided: the path and the pitfall have been shown, the Prophets have sufficiently conveyed the divine command, human reason—which is an internal prophet—is present, the fate of the transgressors and the righteous has been clearly explained, and the "court," the "plaintiff," and the "judge" have been determined. The only thing remaining is the human's firm decision to use the free will Allah has given him to choose the path and move forward. "Ma'ab" means "place of return," and it has also been interpreted as "path or way."

Then, as an emphasis on the issue of the punishment of criminals and to state the proximity of that Great Day for those who consider it far off or a "credit" for the future, He adds: "Indeed, We have warned you of a near punishment" (إِنَّا أَنْذَرْناكُمْ عَذاباً قَرِيباً).

For no matter how long the life of this world may be, compared to the life of the Hereafter, it is no more than an hour. Also, according to the famous Arabic proverb: "Everything that is definitely coming is near" (Kullu ma huwa atin qarib). Thus, in verses 5 to 7 of Surah al-Ma'arij, Allah says to His Prophet: "So be patient with a beautiful patience; indeed, they see it as far off, but We see it as near."

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), also says in this regard: "Everything that is coming is close and near" [1]. Why should it not be near when the primary source of divine punishment is the humans' own deeds, which are always with them? "And indeed, Hell is encompassing the disbelievers" (Ankabut: 54) (وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ‌).

And since on that day a vast group will be drowned in regret and sorrow, becoming remorseful and repentant—a regret that will not benefit them and a repentance that yields no result—following this warning, He adds: "This punishment occurs on the day when man will see what his hands have sent forth, and the disbeliever will say: 'Would that I were dust!'" (يَوْمَ يَنْظُرُ الْمَرْءُ ما قَدَّمَتْ يَداهُ وَ يَقُولُ الْكافِرُ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ تُراباً).

A group of commentators has interpreted the word "yan-zuru" (will see/look) in this verse as "yantaziru" (will wait), saying it means that man on that day will be waiting for the recompense of his deeds. Others have taken it to mean "looking at the record of deeds and observing the good and bad actions." It has also been suggested that it means witnessing the reward and punishment of the deeds.

These interpretations all stem from the fact that they have paid less attention to the issue of the presence and embodiment of human deeds on that day. Otherwise, considering this reality, the meaning of the verse is clear and requires no estimation or figurative interpretation.

To explain: it is understood from various Quranic verses and Islamic narrations that human deeds on that day will be embodied in appropriate forms and appear before him. He will truly see his deeds; he will fall into terror, remorse, and regret upon seeing the sight of his evil deeds, and he will be joyful and happy upon seeing his good deeds. Fundamentally, one of the best rewards for the virtuous and one of the worst punishments for the wicked is these very embodied deeds that will accompany them.

In verse 49 of Surah al-Kahf, we read: "And they will find what they did present" (وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً). And in the final verses of Surah al-Zilzal, it says: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it" (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ‌).

This is one of the wonders of the world of Resurrection: that human deeds are embodied there, and energies are transformed into matter and take life.

The expression "what his hands have sent forth" (ما قَدَّمَتْ يَداهُ) is because humans perform most tasks with their hands, but it certainly is not limited to the deeds of the hands; rather, whatever is done with the tongue, eyes, and ears is also included in this law.

The Quran warns us before that day arrives, saying: "And let every soul look to what it has sent forth for tomorrow" (Hashr: 18) (وَ لْتَنْظُرْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ).

In any case, after the disbelievers see all the deeds of their entire lives present before them, they will be so overwhelmed by sorrow and regret that they will say: "Would that I were dust!" Would that from the beginning I had not progressed beyond the stage of dust, and had not emerged from an inanimate object to a growing plant, from a plant to an animal, and from an animal to a human!

And would that after becoming human and dying, I had never found a new life in the Resurrection after dying and becoming dust! Of course, they know that dust is better than them, for dust receives one seed and sometimes gives back hundreds; dust is the source of various foodstuffs, minerals, and other blessings; dust is the bed of man and the cradle of his life, and it provides all those benefits without causing the slightest harm. But they possess none of the benefits of dust, yet they are the source of many harms.

Yes, the state of a human, who is the noblest of creatures, sometimes reaches a point due to disbelief and sin where he wishes to be in the rank of one of the soulless and lowly beings.

In the verses of the Quran, we read that when the disbelievers and criminals witness the scene of the Resurrection, the judgment of the Lord, and the recompense of deeds, they show various reactions, all of which indicate the intensity of their grief and regret.

Sometimes they say: "Woe to us for this regret, that we neglected our duty toward Allah" (Zumar: 56) (يا حَسْرَتى‌ عَلى‌ ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللَّهِ‌). Sometimes they say: "Our Lord, send us back to the world so that we may do righteous deeds" (Sajdah: 12) (فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً). And sometimes they say: "Would that I were dust and had never been resurrected," as stated in the verses under discussion.

Key Point: A Clear Solution to the Issue of "Determinism and Free Will"

This issue is one of the oldest problems discussed among scholars; one group has advocated for human free will, and another for determinism (Jabr), each mentioning reasons to prove their objective.

However, what is interesting is that both the "determinists" and the "advocates of free will" have, in practice, recognized and accepted the principle of free will and freedom of choice. In other words, all these arguments have been within the sphere of scientific discussions, not in the realm of action. This clearly shows that the principle of freedom of will and choice is innate (fitri) to all humans, and if various sophistries do not interfere, everyone supports the principle of free will.

This universal conscience and common innate nature, which is one of the clearest proofs of free will, manifests in various forms in human life. For if a human considered himself forced in his actions and attributed no choice to himself, why:

  1. Does he sometimes feel regret for actions he has performed or for actions he has not performed, and decide to use past experience in the future? This state of remorse is frequent even for proponents of determinism. If there is no choice involved, why the remorse?
  2. Everyone blames and reproaches evildoers. If it is determinism, why the reproach?
  3. They praise, commend, and glorify the virtuous.
  4. They strive in the education and training of their children so they may become prosperous. If everyone is forced, what meaning does education have?
  5. All scholars, without exception, strive to raise the moral level of society.
  6. A person repents for his mistakes. With the acceptance of the principle of determinism, repentance has no meaning.
  7. A person feels regret over the shortcomings he has had. Why?
  8. Throughout the world, evildoers and criminals are put on trial and subjected to intense interrogation. An act that is outside of one's choice does not warrant interrogation or trial.
  9. Throughout the world and among all peoples, whether God-worshippers or materialists, they believe in punishment for criminals. Punishment for an act one was forced to do?!
  10. Even the proponents of the school of determinism, when someone violates their interests or dignity, cry out, consider him guilty, and drag him to court!

In short, if indeed man has no choice of his own, what does remorse mean? What is the purpose of blame and reproach? Can one reproach someone whose hand is shaking and trembling involuntarily? Why do they praise and encourage the virtuous? Did they have any choice of their own to continue good work through encouragement?

Fundamentally, by accepting the influence of education and training, determinism loses its meaning. Beyond this, moral issues have absolutely no meaning without accepting freedom of will. If we are forced in our actions, what does repentance mean? Why feel regret? Putting a forced person on trial is among the most oppressive acts, and his punishment is worse than his trial.

All of this shows that the principle of freedom of will is innate to all humans and accords with the general conscience of mankind. Not only the common people, but all the elite and all philosophers are like this in practice, and even the determinists are practitioners of free will in action—"The determinists are practitioners of free will from where they do not know!"

Interestingly, the Holy Quran also repeatedly emphasizes this very issue. Not only in the verses under discussion does it say: "So let him who will, take a way of return to his Lord," but in other verses as well, it places great emphasis on human volition and will, the mention of all of which would be lengthy. We suffice with the following three verses:

"Indeed, We guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful" (Dahr: 3) (إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً).

And in verse 29 of Surah al-Kahf, it says: "So let him who will, believe; and let him who will, disbelieve" (فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَ مَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ‌) (but let them know We have prepared for the disbelievers a great fire).

And also in verse 29 of Surah al-Dahr, we read: "Indeed, this is a reminder, so let him who will, take a way to his Lord" (إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا).

The discussion regarding the issue of determinism (Jabr) and delegation (Tafwid) is very long, and books and articles have been written in this field. What was said above was only a look at this issue from the perspective of the "Quran" and "conscience." We conclude this discourse by mentioning an "important point":

The support of a group for the issue of determinism was not only due to some philosophical and argumentative problems; rather, other important psychological and social factors have undoubtedly played a role in the emergence and continuation of this belief.

Many individuals have accepted the belief in "determinism" or "deterministic fate" or "predestination" in its deterministic sense to escape the burden of responsibilities, or they have made this belief a cover for their failures and setbacks resulting from negligence and carelessness. Or it is a cover for their rebellious whims—"God knew from eternity that we would drink wine, and we drink this wine so that Allah's knowledge does not become ignorance!"

Sometimes colonizers, to crush the resistance of the people and extinguish the fire of the nations' wrath, would impose themselves on everyone by resorting to this belief: that your fate was this from the beginning, and what choice is there but submission and contentment?! By accepting this school of thought, the actions of all criminals become justified, the sins of all sinners find a logical excuse, and no difference remains between the obedient and the criminal.

O Allah! Protect us from these deviant beliefs and their consequences. O Lord! On the day when Hell becomes an ambush for the transgressors and Paradise a place of success for the righteous, the eyes of our hope are fixed upon Your grace. O God! On the day when we see all our deeds with our own eyes, do not make us ashamed or disgraced. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Naba
